description: A generic backend/full-stack screening interview. 10 questions covering fundamentals, systems thinking, and behaviour.

questions.question: Tell me briefly about yourself and a project you're proud of.
questions.ideal_answer: A concise 60–90 second pitch: current role, years of experience, primary stack, one concrete project with the problem, their specific contribution, the technical approach, and a measurable outcome (users, latency, revenue, reliability). Structure: context → problem → action → result.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a process and a thread, and when you'd choose one over the other.
questions.ideal_answer: Processes have isolated memory and their own address space; threads share memory within a process. Threads are cheaper to create and communicate via shared memory but require synchronisation. Use processes for isolation/fault-tolerance (e.g. Chrome tabs, worker pools that may crash) and threads for parallel work on shared data with low overhead. Mention GIL in Python as a caveat.

questions.question: What happens, step by step, when I type a URL into my browser and press enter?
questions.ideal_answer: DNS resolution (recursive resolver, caches, root/TLD/authoritative) → TCP handshake (or QUIC) → TLS handshake → HTTP request sent → server processes and responds (possibly hitting load balancer, app server, DB, cache) → browser parses HTML, requests sub-resources, builds DOM/CSSOM, runs JS, renders. Bonus: caching at each layer, HTTP/2 multiplexing.

questions.question: Explain the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases, and give an example of when you'd pick each.
questions.ideal_answer: SQL: relational, structured schema, ACID, strong consistency, good for transactions and complex joins (Postgres for a banking ledger). NoSQL: schema-flexible, horizontally scalable, various models (document, key-value, wide-column, graph); often eventual consistency. Pick NoSQL for high write throughput, flexible schema, or specific access patterns (DynamoDB for a session store, Mongo for evolving product catalog, Redis for cache).

questions.question: How would you design a URL shortener like bit.ly? Walk me through it.
questions.ideal_answer: Clarify scale (reads/writes per second, latency, custom URLs, analytics). API: POST /shorten, GET /:code. Generate short code via base62 of an incrementing ID or hash+collision-check. Store mapping in a KV/SQL store keyed by code. Cache hot codes in Redis. Redirect with 301/302 (302 if analytics matter). Scale reads with cache + read replicas; scale writes with sharding by code. Discuss custom aliases, expiry, abuse prevention.

questions.question: What is the time and space complexity of your favourite sorting algorithm, and why?
questions.ideal_answer: Pick one and be precise. E.g. Quicksort: average O(n log n), worst O(n²) (mitigated with randomised pivot or introsort), space O(log n) for recursion, in-place. Mergesort: guaranteed O(n log n), O(n) extra space, stable. Explain trade-offs and when you'd pick each (mergesort for stability/linked lists, quicksort for in-place with random data).

questions.question: How do you approach debugging a production issue you can't reproduce locally?
questions.ideal_answer: Reproduce the environment differences first (data volume, config, concurrency). Gather signals: logs, metrics, traces, error rates around the incident. Form a hypothesis and test with the smallest safe experiment (feature flag, canary, targeted log). Add structured logging or tracing if missing. Bisect by time or deploy. Communicate status. Post-mortem: root cause, action items, prevention.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a teammate on a technical decision. How did you handle it?
questions.ideal_answer: STAR format. Situation and specific decision. Their view and yours, each with reasoning. How you sought data or a tie-breaker (prototype, benchmark, senior input). The outcome and — critically — what they learned, including any way they were wrong. Shows humility, evidence-based reasoning, and ability to disagree and commit.
common_mistakes: making the other person the villain

questions.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
questions.ideal_answer: Authentication = proving who you are (password, OAuth, passkey, MFA). Authorization = what you're allowed to do once authenticated (RBAC, ABAC, permissions/scopes). AuthN happens first, produces an identity/token; AuthZ is checked on every protected action. Example: logging into GitHub (authN) vs being allowed to push to a repo (authZ).

questions.question: Do you have any questions for me?
questions.ideal_answer: Ask 1–2 thoughtful questions that show research and genuine interest: about the team's biggest current challenge, how success is measured in the first 6 months, engineering culture (code review, on-call, testing), or a specific product/technical decision. Avoid questions easily answered by the website (salary, holidays should come later).
must_cover: at least one specific, thoughtful question
